title: Structure and Randomness for Finite-field Polynomials are (almost) Equivalent
speaker: Guy Moshkovitz
abstract:
What can be said about a system of polynomial equations over a finite field if its number of solutions deviates from that of a random system? Answer: Roughly, one of the polynomials (or a linear combination thereof) can be written in terms of “few” polynomials of lower degree.
What can be said about a polynomial of degree k over a finite field whose derivatives of order k (rather than k+1) are already biased towards 0? Answer: Roughly, it has "large" correlation with some polynomial of lower degree.
The quantitative versions of these questions are important in number theory, additive combinatorics, coding theory, and more, and it is known that they are all related to yet another question: the so-called partition versus analytic rank conjecture for tensors. We will discuss a recent proof of the conjecture(s) up to a logarithmic factor.
The proof has little dependence on prior results (relying on not much more than the Schwartz-Zippel lemma), and at its core is “local rank”, a new, combinatorial notion of tensor rank which we believe could be a powerful tool for studying higher-degree polynomials. 
Joint work with Daniel Zhu.
(No prior background will be assumed in the talk beyond linear algebra)

proof_stages.label: Random benchmark and bias question for finite-field systems
proof_stages.summary: The talk sets up systems of bounded-degree polynomial equations over a finite field, compares their normalized solution density with the random value q^{-m}, and asks which systems have a nonvanishing deviation from that random benchmark.

proof_stages.label: Low-rank reducible structure as the obstruction
proof_stages.summary: A polynomial expressible as a sum of few reducible terms, or a low-rank linear combination of the equations, forces bias, and the classical Bhowmick-Lovett/Schmidt theorem says under very large field-size hypotheses that every biased system has this sort of structure, motivating the n-uniform conjecture.

proof_stages.label: Multilinear tensor reformulation and rank definitions
proof_stages.summary: The system problem is related to a single multilinear polynomial, equivalently a tensor, by Fourier averaging over linear combinations and polarization, and the two central quantities are defined: partition rank as decomposition into reducible multilinear pieces and analytic rank as the exponent of bias.

proof_stages.label: Partition-versus-analytic rank conjecture and elementary comparisons
proof_stages.summary: The central conjecture asserts that partition rank and analytic rank are equivalent with constants depending only on the degree, with the structural direction being that small analytic rank forces small partition rank, while the reverse direction follows from subadditivity and the behavior of reducible tensors.

proof_stages.label: Higher-order Fourier-analysis motivation
proof_stages.summary: Gowers norms and weak correlation norms are introduced as a parallel structure-randomness problem for finite-field functions, and the polynomial phase/Gowers inverse conjectures are explained as translating, in the polynomial-phase case, into analytic-rank versus algebraic-rank questions.

proof_stages.label: Prior results and the logarithmic main theorem
proof_stages.summary: After surveying tower-type, polynomial, large-field, and degree-three results, the speaker states the joint result: in all degrees over all finite fields, partition rank is bounded by a constant times analytic rank with only a logarithmic loss depending on log_q of the analytic rank, yielding near-optimal consequences for the polynomial phase conjecture.

proof_stages.label: Proof strategy via local rank and field extensions
proof_stages.summary: The proof introduces local rank as a combinatorial substitute for algebraic-geometric local smoothness data, proves that it bridges analytic rank and partition rank over polynomially large fields, and then passes to extension fields to handle small base fields at exactly the logarithmic cost in the theorem.

examples.name: Three-by-three determinant tensor and direct sums
examples.note: The determinant trilinear form has maximal partition rank but smaller analytic rank, and direct sums amplify the additive gap, illustrating that the two ranks are not literally equal.
examples.engagement_reason: the speaker explicitly writes the 3-by-3 determinant as a trilinear polynomial, expands it into three reducible pieces to bound partition rank, and discusses direct sums giving ranks 3n versus about 2n

passes.reason: the 3-by-3 determinant tensor is a highly symmetric alternating tensor of maximal partition rank, and its direct sums show partition and analytic rank need not coincide.
The 3-by-3 determinant tensor/direct sums have maximal partition rank but smaller analytic rank, exhibiting an unbounded gap against exact equality/additive closeness.

examples.name: Zero-basepoint local rank as commutative rank
examples.note: At the special point p=0, local rank reduces to maximizing the rank of a linear matrix, recovering the known notion of commutative rank and grounding the new definition.
examples.engagement_reason: the speaker plugs in the zero basepoint in the local-rank definition and traces through why the second coordinate vanishes and the remaining maximum rank is commutative rank

passes.reason: taking the zero basepoint collapses the local-rank definition to the commutative-rank special case, serving as an illustration rather than an obstruction.
